content.text: Every Azure CLI command follows a consistent structure: 'az <group> <command> [flags]'. Once you understand this pattern and how to navigate the group hierarchy, you can discover and use commands for any Azure service without memorising a manual. This section covers the command anatomy, how to explore groups with --help, flag patterns, output formats, and query filtering.

attrs.alt: Azure CLI command anatomy diagram: az (base) + vm (group) + create (subcommand) + --resource-group (flag) + rg-prod (value). Group hierarchy shows az splitting into vm, network, storage, monitor with their subcommands.

content.text: Every Azure CLI command follows this pattern:

az <group> [<subgroup>] <command> [--flag value] [--flag value] ...

Examples:
az vm create ...              → group=vm, command=create
az network vnet create ...    → group=network, subgroup=vnet, command=create
az storage blob upload ...    → group=storage, subgroup=blob, command=upload
az monitor metrics list ...   → group=monitor, subgroup=metrics, command=list
az account show               → group=account, command=show (no subgroup)

The group hierarchy maps directly to Azure service namespaces. If you know the service (VMs, storage, networking), you know the group name. Within a group, the sub-commands are consistent across all groups: create, list, show, update, delete are universal; service-specific actions like start, stop, restart appear where appropriate.

The --help flag works at every level of the hierarchy — on the group, sub-group, or specific command. This is the primary way to discover flags and sub-commands without consulting external documentation.

# Help at the root:
az --help              # lists all top-level groups

# Help on a group:
az vm --help           # lists all vm sub-groups and commands
az network --help      # lists all network sub-commands

# Help on a sub-group:
az network vnet --help # lists all vnet commands (create, list, show, delete, ...)

# Help on a specific command:
az vm create --help    # lists all flags for vm create, marks required ones

# Abbreviated form (same result):
az vm create -h
When az vm create --help lists a flag as '[Required]', omitting it will cause the command to fail. Most required flags can also be satisfied by setting a default with 'az configure --defaults'.
The CRUD operations (create, list, show, update, delete) are consistent across all resource types. Learning them once applies everywhere.
az vm update --name vm-01 --resource-group rg-prod --set tags.env=prod

# CRUD lifecycle for a resource group (same pattern applies to all groups):

# Create
az group create --name rg-demo --location eastus

# List all resource groups
az group list -o table

# Show one resource group
az group show --name rg-demo

# Update (add tags)
az group update --name rg-demo --tags Environment=Demo Owner=alice

# Delete (--yes skips the "are you sure?" prompt)
az group delete --name rg-demo --yes --no-wait
Flags fall into three categories: required (command fails without them), optional (have a default or are not always needed), and global (available on every command).
Global Flags Available on Every Command
Output format: json (default), table, tsv, yaml, jsonc, none
JMESPath query to filter/transform the output
Override the active subscription for this command only
Resource group (common, though not technically global)
Show detailed log output (useful for debugging)
Return immediately without waiting for the operation to complete

# Examples of global flags:

# Table output (readable, no JSON parsing needed):
az vm list -o table

# JSON output (default; good for scripting):
az vm list -o json

# TSV output (tab-separated; good for shell variable assignment):
VM_ID=$(az vm show --name vm-01 --resource-group rg-prod --query id -o tsv)

# YAML output (readable alternative to JSON):
az vm show --name vm-01 --resource-group rg-prod -o yaml

# Debug mode (shows HTTP calls; useful when a command fails unexpectedly):
az vm create --name vm-01 --resource-group rg-prod --image Ubuntu2204 --debug

# No-wait (submit the operation and return immediately):
az vm start --name vm-01 --resource-group rg-prod --no-wait
The -o / --output flag controls how results are presented. Choosing the right format for the context makes the output immediately usable without post-processing.

# json (default) — full object graph; pipe to jq or python for processing:
az vm list -o json | python3 -m json.tool | head -30

# table — human-readable tabular output; columns chosen by CLI:
az vm list -o table
# NAME        RESOURCE GROUP    LOCATION    ZONES
# vm-prod-01  rg-prod           eastus
# vm-dev-01   rg-dev            eastus

# tsv — tab-separated values; perfect for shell variable assignment and loops:
az vm list --query "[].name" -o tsv
# vm-prod-01
# vm-dev-01

# yaml — structured, readable; good for config-like inspection:
az vm show --name vm-prod-01 --resource-group rg-prod -o yaml | head -20

# jsonc — JSON with comments stripped; identical to json for practical purposes

# none — suppress all output (useful in scripts that only care about exit code):
az group create --name rg-test --location eastus -o none
echo "Exit code: $?"
The --query flag accepts JMESPath expressions to filter and transform JSON output before it is rendered. This is more efficient than piping to jq because the filtering happens before serialisation.

# Select a single field:
az vm show --name vm-prod-01 --resource-group rg-prod --query "id" -o tsv

# Select multiple fields as an object:
az vm show --name vm-prod-01 --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "{Name:name, Location:location, Size:hardwareProfile.vmSize}" -o table

# Filter a list by condition:
az vm list --query "[?location=='eastus'].name" -o tsv

# Select nested array fields from a list:
az vm list --query "[].{Name:name, RG:resourceGroup, State:powerState}" -o table

# Count items:
az vm list --query "length(@)" -o tsv

# Get the first item:
az vm list --query "[0].name" -o tsv

# Sort (JMESPath does not sort; use sort_by):
az vm list --query "sort_by(@, &name)[].name" -o tsv

# Find all VMs in a specific resource group:
az vm list \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "[].{Name:name, Size:hardwareProfile.vmSize, Location:location}" \
  -o table

# Get a storage account's primary endpoint:
az storage account show \
  --name stprod001 \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "primaryEndpoints.blob" \
  -o tsv

# Get all role assignments for a resource group:
az role assignment list \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "[].{Principal:principalName, Role:roleDefinitionName}" \
  -o table

# Get all resources tagged with a specific tag:
az resource list \
  --tag Environment=Production \
  --query "[].{Name:name, Type:type, RG:resourceGroup}" \
  -o table
Using Variables and Loops in Scripts
Combining the CLI with shell variables and loops makes it easy to operate on multiple resources without repeating commands.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Restart all stopped VMs in a resource group:

RG="rg-prod"

# Get names of stopped (deallocated) VMs into a variable:
STOPPED_VMS=$(az vm list \
  --resource-group "$RG" \
  --query "[?powerState=='VM deallocated'].name" \
  -o tsv)

if [ -z "$STOPPED_VMS" ]; then
  echo "No stopped VMs in $RG"
  exit 0
fi

echo "Starting VMs in $RG:"
for VM in $STOPPED_VMS; do
  echo "  Starting $VM ..."
  az vm start \
    --name "$VM" \
    --resource-group "$RG" \
    --no-wait
done

echo "All start commands submitted. Use 'az vm list -o table' to verify."

# Apply a tag to all resources in a resource group:
RG="rg-prod"
TAG_KEY="Owner"
TAG_VALUE="alice@contoso.com"

az resource list --resource-group "$RG" --query "[].id" -o tsv | while read -r ID; do
  az resource tag \
    --ids "$ID" \
    --tags "$TAG_KEY=$TAG_VALUE" \
    --is-incremental  # merges with existing tags instead of replacing
  echo "Tagged: $ID"
done
create, list, show, start, stop, restart, delete, resize, update, run-command
vnet, nsg, nic, public-ip, lb, dns
create, list, show, update, delete; vnet subnet add/list
account create/list; blob upload/download/list; sas generate
list, show, tag, move, lock create/list/delete
create, list, show, update, delete, export (ARM template)
metrics list; alert create; activity-log list
create, secret set/show/list; key create; certificate import
create, get-credentials, scale, update, delete; nodepool add
create, list, start, stop, restart, deploy, config set
user show; group member add; sp create-for-rbac; app list
assignment create/list; definition create/list; state summarize
group create/validate/show; sub create; mg create
Finding Commands You Do Not Know

# Search for commands by keyword (finds groups and subcommands):
az find "virtual machine"          # natural language search using AI assistant
az find "blob storage"
az find "assign role"

# List all commands in a group:
az vm --help | grep -E "^  [a-z]"  # parse help output for sub-commands

# Use tab completion to explore:
az network <Tab>                   # shows all network sub-groups
az network vnet <Tab>              # shows all vnet commands
az vm create --<Tab>               # shows all flags for vm create

# Search the CLI reference online:
# https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/reference-index
Working with ARM Deployments via CLI
The 'az deployment' group is one of the most important CLI command groups for infrastructure engineers. It submits ARM templates and Bicep files to Azure for evaluation and deployment.

# Validate a Bicep file before deploying:
az deployment group validate \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters @params.prod.json

# What-if: shows what would change without making changes:
az deployment group what-if \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters @params.prod.json

# Deploy:
az deployment group create \
  --name deploy-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S) \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters @params.prod.json \
  --mode Incremental

# Deploy at subscription scope (for resource groups, policies, RBAC):
az deployment sub create \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file subscription-level.bicep \
  --parameters @sub-params.json

# List recent deployments:
az deployment group list \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "[].{Name:name, State:properties.provisioningState, Time:properties.timestamp}" \
  -o table

# Show deployment operations (find which resource failed):
az deployment group operation list \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --name deploy-20240630-142300 \
  --query "[?properties.provisioningState=='Failed'].{Resource:properties.targetResource.resourceType, Error:properties.statusMessage.error.message}" \
  -o table
Production CLI scripts need error handling, idempotency, and logging. The following patterns make scripts robust enough to run in CI/CD pipelines.
Exit on Error and Strict Mode

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
# -e: exit immediately on non-zero exit code
# -u: treat unset variables as errors
# -o pipefail: catch errors in piped commands

# Check prerequisites before starting:
command -v az >/dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Azure CLI not installed" >&2; exit 1; }
az account show >/dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Not logged in to Azure CLI" >&2; exit 1; }

echo "Starting deployment..."
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters @params.prod.json
echo "Deployment complete."

# Check if a resource group exists before creating:
if ! az group show --name rg-prod >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "Creating rg-prod..."
  az group create --name rg-prod --location eastus
else
  echo "rg-prod already exists, skipping create"
fi

# Idempotent storage account creation:
if ! az storage account show --name stprod001 --resource-group rg-prod >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  az storage account create \
    --name stprod001 \
    --resource-group rg-prod \
    --location eastus \
    --sku Standard_LRS
fi

# Idempotent role assignment (does not fail if assignment already exists):
az role assignment create \
  --role Contributor \
  --assignee alice@contoso.com \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-prod" \
  2>&1 | grep -v "already exists" || true

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Start multiple VMs in parallel (background jobs):
VMS=("vm-web-01" "vm-web-02" "vm-app-01" "vm-app-02")
RG="rg-prod"

for VM in "${VMS[@]}"; do
  az vm start --name "$VM" --resource-group "$RG" --no-wait &
  echo "Start submitted for $VM (PID $!)"
done

# Wait for all background jobs to complete:
wait
echo "All VMs start commands submitted"

# Verify they are running (poll with timeout):
for VM in "${VMS[@]}"; do
  STATE=$(az vm get-instance-view \
    --name "$VM" \
    --resource-group "$RG" \
    --query "instanceView.statuses[1].displayStatus" \
    -o tsv)
  echo "$VM: $STATE"
done
Using az rest for Unlocked ARM API Calls

When a CLI command does not yet exist for a preview feature or a niche API, 'az rest' lets you call any ARM or Microsoft Graph REST endpoint directly with your CLI credentials — no need to obtain a token manually.

# GET request to ARM:
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --uri "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions?api-version=2022-12-01" \
  --query "value[].{Name:displayName, Id:subscriptionId}" \
  -o table

# GET a specific resource (useful for preview API versions):
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --uri "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-prod-01?api-version=2024-03-01" \
  | python3 -m json.tool

# PATCH a resource property not exposed by the CLI:
az rest \
  --method PATCH \
  --uri "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-prod-01?api-version=2024-03-01" \
  --body '{"properties": {"priority": "Spot"}}' \
  --headers "Content-Type=application/json"

# Call Microsoft Graph:
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me" \
  --headers "Content-Type=application/json"

# List all Microsoft.Compute resource types and their API versions:
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --uri "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub>/providers/Microsoft.Compute?api-version=2022-09-01" \
  --query "resourceTypes[].{Type:resourceType, ApiVersions:apiVersions[:3]}" \
  -o table
When a command fails with a cryptic error, the --debug flag shows the full HTTP request and response cycle, which reveals the exact ARM API error message and the endpoint that was called.

# Show full HTTP request/response (very verbose):
az vm create \
  --name vm-test \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --debug 2>&1 | grep -E "(Request|Response|Error)" | head -30

# --verbose shows progress without full HTTP dumps:
az vm create \
  --name vm-test \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --verbose

# Capture only the error message on failure:
az vm create \
  --name vm-test \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  2>&1 | grep -i "error"

# Print the ARM correlation ID (for Microsoft support):
az vm create \
  --name vm-test \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --debug 2>&1 | grep "x-ms-correlation-request-id"

Every Azure CLI command follows the pattern 'az <group> [subgroup] <command> [--flags]'. The CRUD verbs (create, list, show, update, delete) are consistent across all groups. Use '--help' at any level to discover sub-commands and flags. The '-o' flag controls output format — use 'table' for humans, 'tsv' for shell variable capture, and 'json' for full data access. The '--query' flag accepts JMESPath expressions to filter and project fields before rendering. Combine the CLI with shell loops and variables for bulk operations across many resources. Use 'az find' to discover unfamiliar commands by keyword when you know the intent but not the exact group name.
